questions.q: In the functor-of-points picture, a scheme $X$ is encoded by which assignment?
choices: a single set $X(k)$ of $k$-rational points for a fixed field $k$
questions.explain: Yoneda says $X$ is determined by $h_X$. Restricting along $\mathrm{Spec}\dashv\Gamma$ gives the functor $R\mapsto X(R)=\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Spec}\,R,X)$ on commutative rings, which captures $X$ up to unique isomorphism.

choices: the set of maximal ideals of $R$
the set of prime ideals of $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$
questions.explain: For an affine $X=\mathrm{Spec}\,A$, $X(R)=\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(A,R)$. A ring hom out of $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$ is exactly a choice of $(a,b)\in R^2$ satisfying the relation.

questions.q: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$, count $|X(\mathbb{F}_2)|$ by brute force over $a,b\in\{0,1\}$.

hard.q: Let $A = \mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$ and $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$. A ring map $f: A \to B$ is determined by images $f(x) = u, f(y) = v$ in $B$ satisfying $v^2 = u^3 + 1$. Which of the following is a valid pair $(u,v)$ in $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$?
hard.hint: A ring map is determined by images of generators subject to relations.
hard.explain: We need $v^2 = u^3 + 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$. Choice (b): $u=0, v=1$ gives $1 = 0 + 1$ ✓. Choice (a): $0 = t^3 + 1$ is false in a polynomial ring. Choices (c) and (d) similarly fail numerically.

hard.q: Count $|X(\mathbb{F}_3)|$ for $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$ by checking all pairs $(a,b) \in \mathbb{F}_3^2$.
hard.hint: Enumerate $(a, b)$ pairs with $b^2 = a^3 + 1$ over $\mathbb{F}_3$.

hard.q: The correct order of reasoning that proves $h_X \cong h_Y \Rightarrow X \cong Y$ (Yoneda uniqueness) is:
choices: Choose a universal element in $h_X(Y)$, transport it to $h_Y(Y)$ via the natural isomorphism, then show the resulting maps $X\to Y$ and $Y\to X$ are inverses using naturality

Apply the Yoneda lemma with $F = h_Y$: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, h_Y) \cong h_Y(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$, and symmetrically $\mathrm{Nat}(h_Y,h_X)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)$; composites of the two natural isomorphisms are identity natural transformations, hence the corresponding scheme maps compose to identities
Use that $h_X$ and $h_Y$ agree on all fields, so they agree on all rings by Noetherian induction
Observe that $h_X(\mathbb{Z}) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,\mathbb{Z})$ determines $X$ as a topological space
hard.hint: Yoneda gives a map by evaluating at $h_X(X) = \mathrm{id}_X$.
hard.explain: Yoneda gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$ and vice versa. A natural isomorphism $\eta:h_X\xrightarrow{\sim}h_Y$ corresponds to $f:X\to Y$ and $\eta^{-1}$ to $g:Y\to X$; since $\eta^{-1}\circ\eta=\mathrm{id}_{h_X}$ and Yoneda is an isomorphism of sets, $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_X$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_Y$.

questions.q: A functor $F:\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is representable by an affine scheme $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ iff:
questions.hint: Representability by $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ means exactly that $F\cong h_{\mathrm{Spec}\,A}$, i.e.
questions.explain: Representability by $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ means exactly that $F\cong h_{\mathrm{Spec}\,A}$, i.e. $F(R)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(A,R)$ naturally in $R$. The universal element is the image of $\mathrm{id}_A$.

questions.q: Which ring represents the multiplicative group functor $\mathbb{G}_m$ with $\mathbb{G}_m(R)=R^\times$?
questions.hint: A ring hom $\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]\to R$ is exactly a choice of unit of $R$ (the image of $t$ must be invertible).
questions.explain: A ring hom $\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]\to R$ is exactly a choice of unit of $R$ (the image of $t$ must be invertible). So $\mathbb{G}_m=\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$.

questions.q: The functor $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is represented by $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x_{ij}, t]/(t\det(X)-1)$. How many generators (counting $t$) does this ring have for $n=2$?
questions.hint: There are $n^2$ matrix entries $x_{ij}$ and one extra variable $t$ forcing $\det$ invertible.
questions.explain: There are $n^2$ matrix entries $x_{ij}$ and one extra variable $t$ forcing $\det$ invertible. For $n=2$: $4+1=5$ generators.

hard.q: The functor $F(R) = \{f \in R[x] : f \text{ is monic of degree } 2\}$ (monic quadratics over $R$) is representable. Which ring $A$ represents it?
choices: $\mathbb{Z}[a,b]$ — a monic degree-2 poly $x^2 + ax + b$ is a pair $(a,b)$
$\mathbb{Z}[x]$ — polynomials are represented by the affine line
hard.hint: Monic quadratics have two free coefficients: $A = \mathbb{Z}[a_0, a_1]$.
hard.explain: A monic quadratic over $R$ is $x^2 + ax + b$ with $a,b \in R$, determined by the pair $(a,b)$ freely. A ring map $\mathbb{Z}[a,b] \to R$ is exactly such a pair, so $F \cong h_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[a,b]} = \mathbb{A}^2$. No relation is needed since there is no constraint on $(a,b)$.

hard.q: Which of the following functors $\mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}$ is provably NOT representable by any scheme?
choices: $R \mapsto \{\text{idempotents of }R\}/\sim$, where $e\sim e'$ iff $Re\cong Re'$ as $R$-modules
hard.hint: Nonrepresentable functors usually fail descent or Zariski-sheafhood.
hard.explain: The functor in (c) is the functor of rank-1 projective modules; taking isomorphism classes rather than objects destroys the sheaf/descent condition (exactly as with $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$), so it is not representable by a scheme. The others: (a) $= \mathbb{A}^1$, (b) $= \mathbb{A}^1$ again, (d) $= \mathbb{G}_m$.

hard.q: The additive group scheme $\mathbb{G}_a$ represents $R \mapsto (R,+)$ and is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[t]$. Compute $|\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ for $p = 7$.

questions.q: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}:R\mapsto\{\text{elliptic curves over }R\}/\cong$ fails to be representable primarily because:
choices: there are too few elliptic curves over finite fields
elliptic curves have nontrivial automorphisms, so the set-valued functor cannot distinguish twisted families from products
elliptic curves are not algebraic varieties
questions.hint: The automorphism $[-1]$ (and extra ones at $j=0,1728$) lets you build nontrivial families that are pointwise isomorphic to the trivial family but globally distinct.
questions.explain: The automorphism $[-1]$ (and extra ones at $j=0,1728$) lets you build nontrivial families that are pointwise isomorphic to the trivial family but globally distinct. Passing to isomorphism classes forgets this gluing data, breaking the sheaf condition.

questions.q: The fix promoted by stacks is to replace the target category $\mathsf{Set}$ by:
questions.hint: An algebraic stack is (essentially) a sheaf of groupoids $\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Grpd}$.
questions.explain: An algebraic stack is (essentially) a sheaf of groupoids $\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Grpd}$. Remembering automorphisms as 2-arrows restores descent, which taking isomorphism classes destroyed.

questions.q: For an elliptic curve $E$ over a field of characteristic $0$ with generic $j$-invariant, what is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$?
questions.hint: In characteristic $0$ a generic elliptic curve has automorphism group $\{\pm1\}$, of order $2$ (only $j=0$ and $j=1728$ give larger automorphism groups).
questions.explain: In characteristic $0$ a generic elliptic curve has automorphism group $\{\pm1\}$, of order $2$ (only $j=0$ and $j=1728$ give larger automorphism groups).

hard.q: Let $S = \{p_0, p_1\}$ be a two-point base scheme and $E/k$ an elliptic curve. The product family $A = E \times S$ and the twisted family $B$ (glued via $[-1]$ over one point) both have $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(\{p_i\}) = [E]$ for each $i$. Which statement correctly explains why this contradicts representability?
choices: If $\mathcal{M}_{1,1} \cong h_M$, then any $S$-point of $M$ is a morphism $S \to M$ uniquely determined by the functor value on $S$; but $A$ and $B$ are non-isomorphic families giving the same $S$-point, contradicting the bijection $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(S,M)$
The two families disagree at the level of $j$-invariants, so they must come from different points of $M$
A fine moduli space $M$ always has $|M(k)| = |\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(k)|$, which fails for $k = \mathbb{C}$
Stacks do not exist over non-algebraically-closed fields, so the argument is vacuous
hard.hint: Twisting by $[-1] \in \mathrm{Aut}(E)$ gives a torsor that is not trivial globally.
hard.explain: A fine moduli space $M$ with $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\cong h_M$ would give a bijection $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(S,M)$. Both $A$ and $B$ define the same element of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S)$ (same isomorphism class at each point), hence the same map $S\to M$, hence $A\cong B$ by the universal property — but $A \not\cong B$ as families. Contradiction.

hard.q: Elliptic curves with full level-$N$ structure (a choice of basis for $E[N]$) are representable by the modular curve $Y(N)$ for $N\ge 3$. What is the key reason adding level structure restores representability?
choices: Level structure makes the curves proper over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$
Level structure forces $\mathrm{Aut}(E,\alpha_N) = \{\mathrm{id}\}$ (over a base where $N$ is invertible), eliminating the automorphism obstruction
The modular curve $Y(N)$ is affine, unlike $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$
Level structure turns the problem into a linear algebra problem, hence always representable
hard.hint: Level-$N$ kills the automorphism group for $N \ge 3$.
hard.explain: An automorphism of $(E,\alpha_N)$ must fix the level-$N$ basis. For $N\ge 3$ the only automorphism of $E$ that fixes $E[N]$ pointwise is the identity (since $[-1]$ acts as $-1\ne 1$ on $N$-torsion when $N>2$). With trivial automorphisms, the twisted-family obstruction vanishes and the moduli functor is representable.

hard.q: An elliptic curve with $j = 1728$ over $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ has $\mathrm{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$. What is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$?
hard.hint: Automorphisms of $E$ at $j = 1728$ form $\mathbb{Z}/4$; base change to $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ sees them all.
hard.explain: The curve $y^2 = x^3 - x$ (j=1728) has the extra automorphism $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$ of order 4 over a field containing $i$. So $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)| = 4$.

questions.hint: The Yoneda lemma gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, h_Y) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(X,Y)$ naturally, so $X \mapsto h_X$ is fully faithful: every natural transformation between representables comes from…
questions.explain: The Yoneda lemma gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, h_Y) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(X,Y)$ naturally, so $X \mapsto h_X$ is fully faithful: every natural transformation between representables comes from a unique scheme map.

questions.q: Under Yoneda, the universal element of $h_X(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,X)$ that witnesses representability is:
questions.hint: The Yoneda lemma identifies $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F) \cong F(X)$ by evaluating on $\mathrm{id}_X$.
questions.explain: The Yoneda lemma identifies $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F) \cong F(X)$ by evaluating on $\mathrm{id}_X$. So the identity is the universal $X$-point, and every natural transformation is recovered by pushing $\mathrm{id}_X$ forward.

questions.q: Let $F(R) = R \times R$ as a set-valued functor on $\mathrm{CRing}$. Which scheme represents $F$? Enter the integer $n$ such that $F \cong h_{\mathbb{A}^n}$.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{A}^n(R) = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}[x_1,\dots,x_n], R) = R^n$. To get $R \times R = R^2$ we take $n=2$, so $F$ is represented by the affine plane $\mathbb{A}^2$.

hard.q: Suppose $\eta: h_X \Rightarrow h_Y$ is a natural transformation. Unraveling full faithfulness, the correct chain of steps to produce a scheme map $f: X \to Y$ from $\eta$ is:
choices: Evaluate $\eta$ at an arbitrary field $k$, pick any element of $h_X(k)$, and define $f$ by what $\eta_k$ does to it

Apply the Yoneda lemma: $\eta$ corresponds to an element $\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X) \in h_Y(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$; this element is $f$; naturality of $\eta$ recovers $\eta_R$ for all $R$ as $h_Y(-)$ applied to $f$
Take the colimit of $\eta_R$ over all $R$ to assemble a global map
Use that $h_X$ and $h_Y$ are sheaves for the Zariski topology, then apply the gluing axiom to local components of $\eta$
hard.hint: Evaluate the natural transformation at the identity element.
hard.explain: Full faithfulness goes: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\xrightarrow{\sim}h_Y(X)=\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$ by $\eta\mapsto\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X)$. The inverse sends $f$ to the natural transformation whose $R$-component is $h_Y(-)\circ f_* = (-\circ f)$. Naturality is exactly this formula; no choices or colimits are needed.

hard.q: A presheaf $F:\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ lies in the essential image of the Yoneda embedding (i.e., is representable) iff it satisfies which conditions?
choices: $F$ is a sheaf for the Zariski topology and $F$ has a cover by representable open subfunctors with affine overlaps
$F$ sends colimits in $\mathsf{Sch}$ to limits in $\mathsf{Set}$
$F(k)$ is a variety over $k$ for every algebraically closed field $k$
hard.hint: Representability needs the Zariski-sheaf property and local representability.
hard.explain: By Grothendieck's representability theorem (in the affine case: Zariski sheaf + covering by representable opens), a presheaf is a scheme iff it satisfies Zariski descent and is locally isomorphic to affine schemes. Preserving limits is not sufficient: the structure sheaf condition is the key extra datum.

hard.q: Via Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_{\mathbb{A}^1}, h_{\mathbb{A}^2}) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(\mathbb{A}^1, \mathbb{A}^2) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(\mathbb{Z}[x,y], \mathbb{Z}[t])$. A ring map is determined by images $x\mapsto f(t),\, y\mapsto g(t)$. How many such natural transformations correspond to linear maps $x\mapsto t,\, y\mapsto t^2 - 1$? Enter $1$ if this is a valid natural transformation, $0$ otherwise.
hard.hint: Natural transformations correspond to elements of $h_Y(\mathbb{A}^1)$.
hard.explain: A ring map $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]\to\mathbb{Z}[t]$ is determined freely by any images of $x$ and $y$ (no relations in $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]$). So $x\mapsto t,\, y\mapsto t^2-1$ is a valid ring map, hence a valid natural transformation via Yoneda. The answer is $1$.

base-change-interpretation.title: Base change as pullback of functors

questions.q: If $X \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme and $S = \mathrm{Spec}\,k$, then for a $k$-algebra $R$ the base-changed scheme $X_k$ satisfies:
choices: $X_k(R) = X(R)$ viewed only via its $k$-algebra structure on $R$
$X_k(R)$ is always empty when $k$ has characteristic $p$
questions.hint: Base change to $k$ remembers the same functor $X$ but restricted to the slice category of $k$-algebras.
questions.explain: Base change to $k$ remembers the same functor $X$ but restricted to the slice category of $k$-algebras. Equivalently $X_k(R) = X(R)$ where $R$ is regarded with its given $k$-algebra structure — pulling back along $\mathrm{Spec}\,R \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: The functor-of-points identity $(X \times_S Y)(T) = X(T) \times_{S(T)} Y(T)$ says that the fiber product of schemes:
choices: is computed by taking pointwise fiber products of sets
agrees with the disjoint union $X(T) \sqcup Y(T)$
questions.explain: Representable functors preserve limits. So the representable functor of $X \times_S Y$ is the pointwise fiber product of the representable functors of $X, S, Y$ — this is exactly what lets you compute base changes by solving equations over each test ring.

questions.q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. How many elements does $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = X \times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have at the level of $\mathbb{F}_5$-points?
questions.explain: $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = \{a \in \mathbb{F}_5 : a^2 = -1\}$. Since $2^2 = 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$ and $3^2 = 9 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$, there are exactly $2$ solutions.

hard.q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2 - p)$ for a prime $p$. At the special fiber $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p$, the base-changed scheme $X_{\mathbb{F}_p}$ is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$. What kind of scheme is this?
choices: A non-reduced fat point (double point): a single closed point with nilpotent in the structure ring
hard.hint: Base change to $\mathbb{F}_p$: $x^2 \equiv 0 \pmod p$ has the special fiber.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$ is the dual numbers over $\mathbb{F}_p$: it has a unique maximal ideal $(x)$, so a single closed point, but $x$ is nonzero and nilpotent. This is a non-reduced fat point encoding the ramification of $p$ in $X$. The two generic roots $\pm\sqrt{p}$ collide modulo $p$.

hard.q: The Weil restriction $\mathrm{Res}_{\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}}\,\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ represents the functor $R \mapsto \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}(R \otimes_{\mathbb{R}} \mathbb{C}) = R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}$ on $\mathbb{R}$-algebras. Which real scheme represents this functor?
choices: $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{R}}$, since $R$ and $R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}$ have the same underlying set
$\mathbb{A}^2_{\mathbb{R}}$, since $R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}\cong R\times R$ as an $R$-module and the functor sends $R$ to $R^2$
$\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ viewed as a real scheme by restriction of scalars to $\mathbb{R}$
hard.hint: Weil restriction doubles the dimension over $\mathbb{R}$.
hard.explain: As an $\mathbb{R}$-module, $\mathbb{C} \cong \mathbb{R}^2$ (via $a+bi\mapsto(a,b)$), so $R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}\cong R^2$. The functor $R\mapsto R^2$ is $\mathbb{A}^2_{\mathbb{R}}$. This is the classical fact that $\mathrm{Res}_{\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}}\,\mathbb{A}^1$ is $\mathbb{A}^2_{\mathbb{R}}$, explaining why a complex variable has two real degrees of freedom.

hard.q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$ and $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[y]/(y^2+1)$. Using the fiber-product formula $(X\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} Y)(\mathbb{F}_5) = X(\mathbb{F}_5) \times Y(\mathbb{F}_5)$, compute $|(X \times Y)(\mathbb{F}_5)|$.
hard.hint: Count ring maps to each of the factors.
hard.explain: $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = \{2,3\}$ (solutions to $a^2\equiv -1\pmod 5$), so $|X(\mathbb{F}_5)|=2$. Similarly $|Y(\mathbb{F}_5)|=2$. The fiber-product formula gives $|X(\mathbb{F}_5)|\times|Y(\mathbb{F}_5)| = 2\times 2 = 4$.
